docs.title: Welcome
docs.text:
• Easy to Learn!

    Just drag 'n drop, then attach or detach the block.

  • Reactive Environment

    Reactive means action and reaction. When user makes any changes , MNL will response it with the result of evaluation or error if any.

  • Shape and Color

    Helping the user by using visual language capabilities: shape and colour. Shape will represent the grammar and colour for the value type.

  • Type Inference

    The user does not need to declare the type explicitly! Let MNL do it for user (implicit typing).

  • Program Visualization

    Show the beta reduction and the function value visually.

  • It's a Functional PL

    MNL is a pure block-based functional programming language.

  • YAKI

    Currently MNL has a Yet Another [K/C]Compiler Interface a.k.a YAKI as a transpiler from block to text based language such as SML or Scala.

  • Typing Rule

    Another YAKI capability is writing the typing derivation of MNL.

  • Understandable

    In MNL, the terminal (keyword) is a whole word in English, not an abbreviation.

docs.title: About
docs.text:
Macaca Nigra Language [MnL] is a block-based functional programming language. The goal is to help users learn to program. The user can see all processes, such as beta reduction and function value. It also uses visual capabilities such as shape and color to convey meaning to the user.

Some features still need to be improved as a lab language, such as the advanced circularity check (occurrence check) in type inference, reducing the memory used for the tail function and continuation passing style, etc.

docs.title: The Function
docs.text: A function is an expression with a function type and has a name (binding with a name). Lambda is an expression with a function type with no name (anonymous function).

docs.title: The Lambda Block
docs.text:
Fig. 1: The lambda block and binding.

SMLScala 
val mnl_say = fn (hello) => ("MNL: " ^ hello)

val mnl_say = (hello : String) => ("MNL: " + hello)

docs.title: The Function block
docs.text:
MNL simplifies the construction by binding the lambda block with a name in one special declaration block called a function block.

Fig. 2: The function block

SMLScala 
fun greetings () = "Hello there"
val say_it = greetings()

def greetings () : String = "Hello there"
val say_it = greetings()

docs.title: Tail Function
docs.text:
Fig. 3: The tail function

SMLScala 
fun tail_function (n) = if (n <= 1)
    then
        1
    else
        (n * tail_function((n - 1)))

def tail_function (n: Float) : Float = if (n <= 1)
    then
        1
    else
        (n * tail_function((n - 1)))

docs.title: Two or more parameters
docs.text: The core language of MNL is the lambda calculus, which takes one parameter. However, MNL can take two or more inputs as parameters using a tuple or a record. The examples below show how to group two inputs as one parameter.

docs.title: Tuple
docs.text:
Fig. 4: A tuple as the function parameter

SMLScala 
(* SML does not support type inference for a tuple as a parameter. 
Other ML languages may support the syntax below. *)
fun greetings (tpl) = (#1 (tpl)) ^ (#2 (tpl))
val greetings_1 = greetings(("Hello ", "World!"))
val greetings_2 = greetings(("World!", "Hello "))

/* the first item name/ index is 0 in scala */
def greetings (tpl: (String, String)) : String = ((tpl(0)) + (tpl(1)))
val greetings_1 = greetings(("Hello ", "World!"))
val greetings_2 = greetings(("World!", "Hello "))

docs.title: Record
docs.text:
Fig. 5: A record as the function parameter

SMLScala 
 (* SML does not support type inference for a record as a parameter. 
Other ML languages may support the syntax below. *)
fun greetings (rcd) = ((#a (rcd)) ^ (#b (rcd)))
val greetings_1 = greetings({a = "Hello ", b = "World!"})
val greetings_2 = greetings({b = "World!", a = "Hello "})

def greetings (rcd: record of {a : String and b : String}) : String = (/* Scala doesn't have primitive type of 'Record' */ + /* Scala doesn't have primitive type of 'Record' */)
val greetings_1 = greetings(/* Scala doesn't have primitive type of 'Record' */)
val greetings_2 = greetings(/* Scala doesn't have primitive type of 'Record' */)

docs.title: Just Drag, Drop, and Attach the block
docs.text:
To start constructing a program in MNL, drag a block from the toolbox to the playground area and attach it to another block. There is a main block (1) acts as the parent block. The main block will hold declaration block only.

  1. The black block with MNL on the top.

Fig. 1: Drag, drop, and attach the block

docs.title: Declaration
docs.text: A declaration means binding a name to a value. There are two declaration blocks in MNL: variable and function. The function block is a simple model of binding a variable block with a lambda block.

docs.title: Variable
docs.text:
Creating a complete variable block requires an identity block and an expression block. The example below shows how to give a variable_in_MNL name to the string "MNL is easy to learn". When you need the "MNL is easy to learn" value, access the bound variable by name variable_in_MNL.

Fig. 2: Variable binding

SMLScala 
val variable_in_MNL = "MNL is easy to learn"

val variable_in_MNL = "MNL is easy to learn"

docs.title: Expression
docs.text: Expression is a value. Every expression blocks have its value including operators, constants, and constructor.

docs.title: Operator
docs.text:
There are two types of primitive operators based on input: unary (1) and binary (2) operator.

  1. Take one input.
  2. Take two inputs.

docs.title: Unary
docs.text:
Fig. 3: Boolean Operator Not

SMLScala 
(not false)

(!false)

Fig. 4:Boolean Operator Not - binding

SMLScala 
val unary_operator = (not false)

val unary_operator = (!false)

docs.title: Binary
docs.text:
Fig. 5: Arithmetic Operator

SMLScala 
(111 + 222)

(111 + 222)

Fig. 6: Arithmetic Operator - binding

SMLScala 
fun increment (n) = (1 + n)
val three = increment(2)

def increment (n: Float) : Float = (1 + n)
val three = increment(2)

docs.title: Selection
docs.text:
Fig. 7: Selection

SMLScala 
(* SML does not support type inference for a tuple as a parameter. 
Other ML languages may support the syntax below. *)
fun the_greater (pair) = if ((#1 pair) < (#2 pair))
  then
    (#2 pair)
  else
    (#1 pair)
val two_or_three = the_greater((2, 3))

def the_greater (pair: (Float, Float)) : Float = if ((pair(0)) < (pair(1)))
  then
    (pair(1))
  else
    (pair(0))
val two_or_three = the_greater((2, 3))

docs.title: Sequence
docs.text:
A sequence is a collection of expressions that are evaluated sequentially. The last expression is the type and value of a sequence block.

Fig. 8: Sequence

SMLScala 
val sequence = (
    "Hi, there!";
    "I am MNL"
  )

val sequence = (() =>{
    "Hi, there!"
    "I am MNL"
  })()

docs.title: Pattern Matching
docs.text:
Fig. 9: Pattern Matching

SMLScala 
fun translator_good_morning (lang_code) = case lang_code
  of "en" => "Good Morning"
  | "de" => "Guten Morgen"
  | "jp" => "おはよう"
  | "id" => "Selamat Pagi"
  |  _   => "Unknown"

val greetings = translator_good_morning("de")

def translator_good_morning (lang_code: String) : String = lang_code match
  case "en" => "Good Morning"
  case "de" => "Guten Morgen"
  case "jp" => "おはよう"
  case "id" => "Selamat Pagi"
  case  _   => "Unknown"

val greetings = translator_good_morning("de")

docs.title: Let-in
docs.text:
Let-in is a local binding of declaration(s) in expression.

Fig. 10: Let-in

SMLScala 
val let_in = let
    val hello = "Hello "
    fun speech (name) = (hello ^ name)
  in
    speech("MNL")
  end

val let_in = (() => {
  val hello = "Hello "
  def speech (name: String) : String = (hello + name)

  speech("MNL")
})()

docs.title: Return a function
docs.text:
SMLScala 
  fun f_continuation (param_1) = fn (param_2) => fn (param_3) => (param_1 ^ (param_2 ^ param_3) )
  val first_app = f_continuation("Hello ")
  val second_app = first_app("World")
  val the_result = second_app("!")

  def f_continuation (param_1: String) : (String) => (String) => String = (param_2 : String) => (param_3 : String) => (param_1 + (param_2 + param_3) )
  val first_app = f_continuation("Hello ")
  val second_app = first_app("World")
  val the_result = second_app("!")

docs.title: Function as a parameter
docs.text:
SMLScala 
    fun fold_list (list_a) = fn (accumulator) => fn (operator) => let
      fun supporter (list_b) = if null(list_b)
        then
        accumulator
        else
        operator(hd(list_b) ) (supporter(tl(list_b) ) )
    in
      supporter(list_a)
    end
    fun sum (a) = fn (b) => (a + b)
    val application_of_fold = fold_list([17, 2, 2004]) (0) (sum)

/* Scala doesn't support type variable on lambda */

docs.title: The List
docs.text: The list type is a container type with a collection of homogeneous member types.

docs.title: Empty list
docs.text:
Fig. 1: The empty list block

SMLScala 
[]

List()

Fig. 2: Connecting the empty list block to the variable block

SMLScala 
val list_constructor = []

val list_constructor = List()

docs.title: Non-empty list
docs.text:
Fig. 3: Constructing the non-empty list block

SMLScala 
["my name ", "is ", "MNL"]

List("my name ", "is ", "MNL")

Fig. 4: Connecting the non-empty list block to the variable block

SMLScala 
val list_constructor = ["my name ", "is ", "MNL"]

val list_constructor = List("my name ", "is ", "MNL")

docs.title: is empty
docs.text:
Fig. 5: The is-empty block operator with the empty list block

SMLScala 
val list_constructor = null([])

val list_constructor = List().isEmpty

Fig. 6: The is-empty block operator with the non-empty list block

SMLScala 
val list_constructor = null([])

val list_constructor = List().isEmpty

docs.title: head
docs.text:
SMLScala 
hd(["My name ", "is ", "MNL"])

List("My name ", "is ", "MNL").head

docs.title: tail
docs.text:
SMLScala 
tl(["My name ", "is ", "MNL"])

List("My name ", "is ", "MNL").tail

docs.title: append
docs.text:
SMLScala 
("Hello, " :: ["My name ", "is ", "MNL"])

("Hello, " :: List("My name ", "is ", "MNL"))

docs.title: Sum
docs.text:
SMLScala 
fun sum_list (list_a) = if null(list_a)
  then
    0
  else
    (hd(list_a)  + sum_list(tl(list_a)))

val sum_list_application = sum_list([17, 2, 200, 4])

def sum_list (list_a: List[Float]) : Float = if (list_a.isEmpty)
  then
    0
  else
    ((list_a.head)  + sum_list((list_a.tail)))

val sum_list_application = sum_list(List(17, 2, 200, 4))

docs.title: Workspace
docs.text:
The MnL workspace is devided into three parts: Menu, Toolbox and Top-level block, and Playground.

Fig. 1: Workspace

docs.title: Menu
docs.text:
File: start a new workspace, save and download blocks on the playground, or upload and load blocks into playground.

Theme: change the theme of workspace to dark or light mode.

Basic Example: load basic examples such as the identity function, the recursive function, etc.

Advanced Example: load advanced example such as the filter, fold, or fmap function.

Program Visualization and Transpiler: show/ hide window of the function value, beta reduction, or transpiler.

Usage: go to the user guide website.

docs.title: Toolbox and Top-level block
docs.text:
Toolbox icon: clickable icon to show or hide toolbox.

Search: search block by name.

Top-level block: used as the parent block for the program construction..

docs.title: Playground
docs.text:
Trash: hold all deleted blocks.

Zoom in/out: Zoom in/ out the play area.

Rest: reset the play area position.

Play Area: holds all blocks.

docs.title: Toolbox
docs.text:
Toolbox groups the blocks based on the language grammar. To show the block in the category, click the category. There are seven categories on the top; the other seven below are part of the expression. The user can also search the block from the search box located at the bottom of the toolbox.

Fig. 2: Toolbox

docs.title: Block Anatomy
docs.text:
Fig. 3: Horizontal input/output block

Fig. 4: Vertical input/output + horizontal input block

Gear Icon: show the add/remove block input toolbox.

Exclamation Mark Icon: Debugger, shows the error message.

Question Mark Icon: The Info, shows the block information: block category, type, and value.

Suggestion Box Color: shows the colors of blocks that can be connected.

The Input Notch: shows the shape of the required output notch to attach to the block.

The Output Notch: used as a hook to another block.

docs.title: Add/Remove Input
docs.text:
The number of input block on some blocks can be adjust dynamically using add/remove toolbox or add/remove icon.

Fig. 5: Add/remove input toolbox

Fig. 6: Add/remove input using icon

docs.title: Block Info
docs.text:
The Info shows block information such as block category based on the grammar, term type, and value. tester

Fig. 7: The Info

docs.title: Debugger
docs.text:
The debugger shows the requirement that must be fulfilled to make a complete block (1), or the term-type error.

  1. A complete block is a block with all requirements fulfilled, ex.: The Info.

Fig. 8: Debugger

docs.title: Program Visualisation
docs.text:
MNL has an interpreter called Tarsius. Tarsius was built with program visualization in mind, meaning all evaluations are done in the visual workspace. This is in contrast to a text-based programming language, where it is difficult to show the function value or the process of function application (beta reduction).

When the expression block type is a function, users can check the function value's content by right-clicking on the block and choosing Function Value from the block menu.

Fig. 1: The function value's menu

Fig. 2: The content of function's value

Users can show the beta reduction process by right-clicking on the application block and choosing Beta Reduction. In the beta reduction window, they can see the substitution step arranged sequentially from top to bottom and check the value of the block.

Fig. 3: The beta reduction's menu

Fig. 4: Beta reduction

docs.title: Constructor
Projection
docs.text:
SMLScala 
(#str {bool = false, str = "is", num = 0})

/* Scala doesn't have primitive type of 'Record' */
SMLScala 
val record_constructor = {bool = false, str = "is", num = 0}
val projection = (#str record_constructor)

/* Scala doesn't have primitive type of 'Record' */

docs.title: The Transpiler
docs.text:
MNL has its own transpiler called YAKI (Yet Another [K/T]Transpiler Interface), which transforms block-based programming languages into text-based languages such as SML and Scala. Beyond the transpiling, YAKI was able to write a typing derivation of MNL.

Right-click on the workspace and choose Transpiler to open the transpiler window. The combo box at the bottom allows users to select the target language.

Fig. 1: YAKI

docs.title: Example: The Identity Function
docs.text:
The Identity Function

The identity function is a function that returns the input value.

Fig. 2: The identity function

Typing DerivationSMLScala

identity_function.sml
fun f_identity (a) = a
identity_function.scala
def f_identity [B] (a : B) : B = a

docs.title: Constructor
docs.text:
SMLScala 
(true, "is", 1)

(true, "is", 1)
SMLScala 
val tuple_constructor = (true, "is", 1)

val tuple_constructor = (true, "is", 1)

docs.title: Projection
docs.text:
SMLScala 
val tuple_constructor = (true, "is", 1)
val projection = (#1 tuple_constructor)

val tuple_constructor = (true, "is", 1)
val projection = (tuple_constructor(1))

docs.title: Type Suggestion
docs.text:
One of the MNL capability is type suggestion. MNL will show the suggestion type by coloring the rounded-box near to the input notch.

Initially, MNL will color the suggestion box with any type color (dark silver), or when the type is fixed, then the color is based on it.

Fig. 1: Suggestion type on the incomplete block

However, when the block has some attached input (The partially completed block (1) ), MNL will construct the type based on the type constraint rule and update the suggestion box color. The example below shows the color of suggestion box on the input notch.

  1. The partially completed block is a block with some attached input blocks, but not all input notches have connected with the input block.

Fig. 2: The partially completed block

When the block is complete, user can check whether the input block complies with the typing rule. For example, the completed block below, the input block attached to the otherwise input, does not comply with the typing rule. The typing rule required the block with the green color (2), but found the block with the yellow color (3).

  1. The completed block is a block with all input notches have connected to the input block.
  2. The green color is the color for the type string.
  3. The yellow color is the color for the type number.

Fig. 3: The completed block

When the type constraint builder can't find the type from the attached input block but the block has parent block, it will infer the type from the parent block.

Fig 4: Infer from the parent block

The type constraint builder tries to infer the type from the attached input block first, then the parent block.

Fig. 5: From the attached block, then the parent block
